title: `swapExactJarForJar()` trusts attacker-supplied jars and converter calldata, exposing controller-held tokens to theft
claim: The public jar-swap entrypoint never validates that `_fromJar` and `_toJar` are protocol-controlled jars, yet it trusts their `token()`, `withdraw()`, `deposit()`, `balanceOf()` and `transfer()` behavior. A malicious `_toJar` can therefore receive an approval for any controller-held `_toJarToken` balance and steal it during `deposit(_toBal)`. Separately, the same function lets callers run arbitrary calldata against any governance-whitelisted converter via `delegatecall`, and the bundled helper contracts include direct sweep/approval gadgets such as `refundDust()` and `add_liquidity()` that operate in controller context.
impact: Any ERC20 balance resident on the controller can be permissionlessly drained. This includes accidental transfers, residual dust from prior operations, and any tokens left on the controller for later recovery. The attack is zero-capital in the fake-jar path and does not require compromising governance once the function is deployed.
paths: Deploy a fake `_fromJar` that tolerates zero-amount calls and a malicious `_toJar` whose `token()` returns a target ERC20 currently held by the controller and whose `deposit(uint256)` pulls the approved balance to the attacker; then call `swapExactJarForJar(fakeFromJar, maliciousToJar, 0, 0, [], [])`.

If a bundled proxy helper has been approved in `approvedJarConverters`, call `swapExactJarForJar(validOrFakeJar, validOrFakeJar, 0, 0, [approvedHelper], [craftedCalldata])` and use `refundDust()` or `add_liquidity()` to transfer or approve controller-held balances to an attacker-controlled recipient/contract.

title: Permissionless harvests are sandwichable because reward swaps and reinvestment use zero minimums
claim: `onlyBenevolent` explicitly permits any EOA to call `harvest()`, and the shared `_swapUniswap()` helper hardcodes `amountOutMin = 0`. The affected harvest implementations also add Uniswap or Curve liquidity with zero minimum LP-out / component-out protections, so a searcher can move the relevant pools immediately before harvest, force poor execution, and back-run to restore price.
impact: Harvested rewards can be systematically skimmed by MEV searchers, reducing strategy yield and jar PPS growth for all depositors. Although user principal is not directly transferred to the attacker, repeated public harvests can leak a meaningful fraction of strategy revenue over time.
paths: Wait for a strategy to accrue CRV, UNI, COMP, or SNX rewards, then front-run a public `harvest()` with price manipulation in the reward swap path and back-run after the zero-minimum trade executes.
For Curve and UNI LP strategies, additionally skew the pool used during the reinvest step so `add_liquidity(..., 0)` mints materially fewer LP tokens before unwinding the manipulation.

title: Public LP conversion contracts expose user principal to MEV because every leg uses zero minimums
claim: `PickleSwap.convertWETHPair()` and `UniCurveConverter.convert()` remove liquidity, swap components, and add liquidity back with all minimum outputs set to zero and without any caller-specified slippage bounds. Because these functions are public and operate on the caller's deposited LP tokens, they force the user to accept whatever execution price is available at inclusion time.
impact: A sandwich attacker can extract value directly from user principal during LP-to-LP or LP-to-Curve migrations, not merely from harvested rewards. Large conversions can lose a meaningful percentage of the user's assets in a single transaction.
paths: Victim submits `convertWETHPair()` for a valuable WETH pair; attacker front-runs to skew the source or destination pair price, the victim removes/adds liquidity and swaps at manipulated zero-minimum rates, and the attacker back-runs to capture the difference.

Victim calls `UniCurveConverter.convert()`; attacker manipulates the WETH/stable route and/or the Curve pool before inclusion so the victim mints far fewer `scrv` tokens than fair value, then unwinds immediately afterward.

title: `SCRVVoter.deposit()` is permissionless and can route voter-controlled tokens into an attacker-chosen gauge
claim: Unlike `withdraw()`, `withdrawAll()`, `harvest()`, `claimRewards()`, and `vote()`, `SCRVVoter.deposit()` lacks the `strategies[msg.sender]` authorization check. Any caller can therefore pick arbitrary `_token` and `_gauge` values, move all matching `_token` balance from `SCRVVoter` into `CRVLocker`, approve `_gauge` for the locker's full balance of that token, and invoke `_gauge.deposit(_balance)` via `CRVLocker.execute()`.
impact: Any compatible ERC20 balance sitting on either `SCRVVoter` or `CRVLocker` becomes permissionlessly steerable into an attacker contract that implements `deposit(uint256)` and pulls the approved tokens. This can drain stray balances, temporary holdings, or any future tokens that accumulate on these helper contracts outside the normal happy path.
paths: Wait for or send a token balance to `SCRVVoter`, then call `SCRVVoter.deposit(attackerGauge, targetToken)` where `attackerGauge.deposit(uint256)` performs `transferFrom(address(crvLocker), attacker, amount)` on `targetToken`.
If `CRVLocker` already holds a balance of `targetToken`, the same call approves and exposes the locker's full balance, not just the amount moved from `SCRVVoter` in that transaction.

title: The timelock delay can be bypassed for the first admin handoff
claim: The constructor leaves `admin_initialized = false`, and `setPendingAdmin()` explicitly allows one direct call from the current `admin` before later calls must come from the timelock itself. If deployment or initialization ever leaves the bootstrap admin in place longer than intended, that admin can replace governance without queuing a delayed transaction.
impact: In the narrow window before the first `setPendingAdmin()` call, the contract's normal timelock guarantee does not apply to the admin handoff. A compromised or malicious bootstrap admin could seize permanent control immediately instead of waiting through the configured delay.
paths: While `admin_initialized == false`, the current `admin` calls `setPendingAdmin(attacker)` directly.
The attacker then calls `acceptAdmin()` and gains all timelock-controlled privileges without any queued delay.
